hypotheses: Branding alters the associations between products and attitude functions.
Products associated with utilitarian attitudes at the category level support more symbolic attitudes at the brand level, and vice versa.
claim.statement: Branding alters attitude functions and reduces the advantage of function-matching persuasive appeals.

data.sample_size: Various, range from 54 to 206 participants per study
data.notes: The study uses fictional and real brands; multiple smaller studies aggregated into findings.

method.description: The study conducted experiments where participants evaluated the function of attitudes towards products at both category and brand levels, using hypothetical and real advertising appeals to assess persuasion effectiveness.
steps: Select product categories for utilitarian and symbolic attitudes.
Assign participants to evaluate products at category or brand level.
Manipulate branding and measure attitude functions.
Deliver utilitarian and symbolic appeals and measure persuasion effectiveness.
method.independent_variables: Branding, type of appeal (utilitarian or symbolic)

results.summary: Branding causes attitudes associated with products to become less aligned with the category's dominant attitude function. Utilitarian products become more symbolic when branded and symbolic products become more utilitarian. The effectiveness of function-matching persuasive appeals is reduced at the brand level.

metadata.original_paper_title: Branding Alters Attitude Functions and Reduces the Advantage of Function-Matching Persuasive Appeals
